rulings.Case Title: DORAN CONSTRUCTION & DESIGN INC. VS. ROBERT POYNTER
rulings.Calendar Matter: Nili Poynter'S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Response To Interrogatory, And For Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Monday, May 17, 2021, Line 1, DEFENDANTS ROBERT POYNTER AND NILI POYNTER's Motion To Compel Response To Interrogatory, And For Monetary Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Aaron Minnis, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Denied. As drafted, Special Interrogatory No. 419 asks the responding party (DCD) to provide information for every line item in 59 invoices, which is overbroad on its face and would necessitate the preparation or the making of a compilation, abstract, audit, or summary of entries in files and records of DCD. DCD's answer complies with CCP 2030.230, in that it refers to the statute and specifies the writings from which the answer may be derived or ascertained-that is, the 59 bundles of documents that DCD produced, with each bundle containing an invoice and all numbered documents that support the charges on that invoice. DCD's specification provides sufficient detail to permit Poynter to locate and to identify, as readily as DCD can, the documents from which the answer may be ascertained on an invoice-by-invoice basis. To the extent there may be confusion about particular invoices or line items, counsel could have engaged in further meet and confer to resolve such confusion prior to filing the instant motion and/or narrowed the requests. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link (DISCOVERY, DEPT 301 @ 9:00AM), or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to aaron@minnisandsmallets.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If parties have arranged for a court reporter to appear, please include the following in your email: (1) ensure that they are California Certified Shorthand Reporters; (2) the reporter's full contact information [name, CSR number, personal work email, and phone number]. If the reporter is not a California Certified Shorthand Reporter, the transcript cannot be certified for use in California courts. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must e-mail it to the Judge Pro Tem. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: NATHAN PETER RUNYON VS. PAYWARD, INC., A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion In Support Of Motion To Compel Deposition Of Witness, Production Of Responsive Documents And For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Monday, May 17, 2021, Line 2, DEFENDANTS PAYWARD, INC. D/B/A KRAKEN AND KAISER NG's Motion In Support Of Motion To Compel Deposition Of Witness, Production Of Responsive Documents And For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Aaron Minnis, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Granted, in part. The motion to compel the deposition of nonparty witness Employee 5 is granted. Within 10 days, counsel for Runyon, Payward and nonparty witness Employee 5, shall meet and confer in good faith and select a date no later than June 30, 2021, for Employee 5 to be deposed. Payward's request for sanctions is denied. Payward fails to demonstrate that opposing parties acted without substantial justification, or engaged in spoliation of evidence, or that opposing parties' actions were in made in bad faith, frivolous or solely intended to cause unnecessary delay. CCP 128.5(a). As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link (DISCOVERY, DEPT 301 @ 9:00AM), or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to aaron@minnisandsmallets.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If parties have arranged for a court reporter to appear, please include the following in your email: (1) ensure that they are California Certified Shorthand Reporters; (2) the reporter's full contact information [name, CSR number, personal work email, and phone number]. If the reporter is not a California Certified Shorthand Reporter, the transcript cannot be certified for use in California courts. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must e-mail it to the Judge Pro Tem. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ANA MORLEY VS. STEVEN P. OKUHN
rulings.Calendar Matter: Corrected Version Of The March 31, 2021, Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Responses To Discovery Requests And Impose Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Monday, May 17, 2021, Line 4, PLAINTIFF ANA MORLEY's Corrected Version Of The March 31, 2021, Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Responses To Discovery Requests And Impose Sanctions (Part 1 of 2 for purposes of entry of Tentative Ruling). Pro Tem Judge Aaron Minnis, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Granted, in part. The motion is timely because it was electronically served on March 31, 2021, the last date per the parties' stipulation. See Young Decl Para. 13:15-18. The motion is granted as to requests for production relating to the parties' jointly-titled financial statements; the Macondray residence; the parties jointly-titled accounts, assets, and liabilities; and communications by or between the parties relating thereto. Within 30 days, Defendant shall provide verified, Code-compliant responses and produce documents. Where no documents exist, Defendant shall affirm that a diligent search and a reasonable inquiry has been made. CCP 2031.230. Defendant shall provide a privilege log and counsel for the parties shall promptly meet and confer regarding a protective order. As to the remaining requests, the motion is denied without prejudice. The requests are overbroad and seek vast information about every facet of Defendant's finances individually, and in some instances the finances of third parties, which is premature given the pleadings are to be amended, the absence of a protective order, and the lack of sufficient meet and confer by counsel to narrow the issues in dispute prior to filing the instant motion. Moreover, a right of privacy exists as to a party's or third party's confidential financial affairs, even when the information sought is admittedly relevant to the litigation. Cobb v. Sup.Ct. (Tleel) (1979) 99 CA3d 543, 550. The party seeking discovery must show a particularized need for the confidential information sought and that the information is directly relevant to a cause of action or defense, i.e., that it is essential to determining the truth of the matters in dispute (Britt v. Sup.Ct. (San Diego Unified Port Dist.) (1978) 20 C3d 844, 859-862), which Plaintiff has not shown. The request for sanctions is denied. (Tentative Ruling continues in Part 2 of 2) =(302/JPT)
